journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of magnetic resonance imaging : JMRI

title-group.article-title: Evaluation of Image-Level Harmonization Methods for Multi-Center MR Neuroimaging

contrib.collab: for the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative

article-meta.aff: 1 Department of Radiology, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, United States
2 Department of Neurology and Neurological Sciences, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, United States

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author Information: Greg Zaharchuk, Department of Radiology, Stanford University, Stanford, CA, USA, 1201 Welch Rd, Stanford, CA 94305, gregz@stanford.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Multi-center imaging studies create large-scale data that are useful for identifying pathological patterns and robust training of deep learning models. However, variation due to site and scanner differences can confound analyses, emphasizing the need for harmonization.

sec.title: Purpose:
sec.p: To evaluate scanner-related differences in T1w and T2-FLAIR images in the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) dataset and assess the performance of publicly available image-level harmonization tools.

sec.title: Study Type:
sec.p: Retrospective.

sec.title: Population:
sec.p: Scanner group analysis: 1143 ADNI3 subjects (233 GE, 173 Philips, 250 Siemens, with 487 Siemens subjects used as an independent reference group). Within-subject comparison: paired multi-vendor scan sessions from 8 subjects.

sec.title: Field Strength/Sequence:
sec.p: 3.0T, T1w and T2-FLAIR MRI sequences.

sec.title: Assessment:
sec.p: Gray/white matter contrast ratio (G/W ratio), white matter hyperintensity (WMH) volume, and image feature similarity metrics (Fréchet Inception Distance [FID], Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity [LPIPS]) were compared across scanner vendors before and after harmonization with statistical (ComBat) and deep learning (HACA3) algorithms.

sec.title: Statistical Tests:
sec.p: One-way ANOVA and post-hoc Games-Howell tests were conducted to assess differences between scanner groups across image pipelines (baseline, post-harmonization). Repeated-measures ANOVA and post-hoc paired t-tests with Bonferroni correction were used to evaluate similarity metric changes pre- and post-harmonization for multi-vendor subjects. We defined statistical significance as p<0.05.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: At baseline, significant image differences in G/W ratio and WMH volumes between vendors were identified. Both ComBat and HACA3 harmonization improved G/W ratio consistency for T1w and T2-FLAIR imaging across vendors, particularly for GE T2-FLAIRs. HACA3 led to the best similarity between scanner datasets: [mean FID T1w/T2-FLAIR: 10.45/14.62 (Baseline); 7.45/11.71 (ComBat); 5.60/8.91 (HACA3)]. Only HACA3 harmonization resulted in non-significant differences between vendors for WMH volume.

sec.title: Data Conclusion:
sec.p: HACA3 deep learning harmonization outperformed a statistical method, ComBat, improving MR contrast consistency and feature similarity across vendors. However, difficulties in harmonizing T2-FLAIRs highlight limitations in current multi-contrast MR harmonization tools.

sec.title: Technical Efficacy:
sec.p: Stage 1

abstract.title: Plain Language Summary
abstract.p: Scanning patients across multiple sites is essential for large data collection, yet variation in scanner technology may limit interpretability. We compared the performance of statistical and AI harmonization methods to improve consistency between scanner vendors. We found that the AI model (HACA3) led to the greatest similarity between scanner groups, yet challenges remained with harmonizing a particular imaging sequence. As AI models offer insight into disease patterns through training on large amounts of data, refinement of standardized harmonization tools is an important step to avoid the effects of scanner differences.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Large, multi-center, longitudinal imaging research studies can improve our understanding of normal aging and disease pathology and progression (1, 2). These collections, often numbering in the thousands or tens of thousands of imaging instances, have also become a key resource for the training and testing of advanced machine learning (ML) models, such as for disease identification, prognostication, segmentation, and image generation (3). However, one of their greatest strengths, the incorporation of a diverse range of scan settings and scanners, can also be a liability; imaging data quality may be highly variable due to data collection across various sites’ scanner vendors, which use different hardware and pulse sequence parameters. This can make interpretation of results difficult due to the potential confounding of site and biological effects (4). Thus, imaging harmonization to limit inter-scanner variability may help improve the analysis of such multi-center studies.

Harmonization is a fundamental application in multi-site research studies like clinical trials which aim to collect large amounts of data while preserving pathological information across sites (5). Harmonized data also supports the advancement of deep learning models, with generalizability remaining a major issue as evidenced by underperformance across scanner vendors and image protocols not included in the training set (6). Statistical harmonization methods such as ComBat, originally developed for genomic data, have shown to successfully remove inter-site variability for image-derived metrics such as cortical thickness and diffusion tensor imaging data (7–9). With the advent of deep learning, more sophisticated harmonization tools aim to directly harmonize image voxels, seeking to maintain individual anatomical details while removing site or scanner effects (4, 10, 11).

The purpose of this study was to evaluate the performance of current, publicly available harmonization methods in a large multi-center dataset (the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative 3 [ADNI3]).

sec.title: Subject Cohort and Selection Criteria
sec.p: The data used in this study was acquired from the public, de-identified ADNI database, in which written informed consent was obtained from all participants. In the ADNI study, standardized scan protocols for each scanner type are mandated with the goal of image harmonization. ADNI3 (2016–2022) was the most recently completed phase of the study, in which improvements were made to the data collection, including a universal 3D acquisition for T2-FLAIR imaging. All MRI scans were acquired at 3T under defined ADNI scan protocols (adni.loni.usc.edu): T1w (Geometry: 208×240×256 mm @1×1×1 mm, TE: min full echo, TR: 2300 ms, TI: 900 ms), T2-FLAIR (Geometry: 256×256×160 mm @1.2×1×1 mm, TE: 119 ms, TR: 4800 ms, TI: 1650 ms).

For Part 1 of this study, we sought to evaluate differences due to scanner type by identifying ADNI3 subjects with T1w and T2-FLAIR images acquired on three different scanner manufacturers (GE, Philips, Siemens). If the subjects had multiple eligible scans, only the most recently collected session was included. Because the majority of subjects identified were scanned on Siemens scanners, it was chosen as the reference scanner type for harmonization. Due to the large number of subjects scanned on Siemens scanners, we used kernel density estimation (KDE)-based probabilistic sampling to select 250 Siemens subjects that were age-matched to the GE and Philips groups. The remaining subjects imaged on a Siemens machine were used as the target reference group for harmonization (Figure 1).

For Part 2 of the study, we identified individuals with sequential studies on different scanner manufacturers, obtained within 3 years. For example, a subject who first had a scan on a GE scanner and a subsequent scan on a Philips scanner. All details of study sample selection for Parts 1 and 2 of the study are presented in Figure 1.

Using these criteria, we obtained the following cohort: scan sessions from 233 GE, 173 Philips, and 250 Siemens ADNI3 subjects were included in Part 1 of the study. One subject in the GE group (81 years old, female, mild cognitive impairment [MCI]) had processing issues during HACA3 harmonization (missing brain tissue post-harmonization) and was excluded from the group analyses. The remaining 487 Siemens subjects were utilized as an independent reference group as the target contrast batch for the harmonization tools. For Part 2 of the study, we identified 8 subjects scanned on two different scanner vendors (1 GE/Philips, 3 Philips/Siemens, 4 GE/Siemens) (Table I).

sec.title: Pre-processing
sec.p: All MRI scans underwent correction for low frequency intensity non-uniformity using N4BiasFieldCorrection (12). T2-FLAIR images were co-registered to the T1w images using the Advanced Normalization Tools (ANTs) (13), and both T1w and T2-FLAIR images were subsequently spatially normalized to the MNI152–1mm space using antsApplyTransforms. Next, we applied two previously reported harmonization methods (a statistical method [ComBat] (7–9) and a deep learning method [HACA3] (4)) to improve inter-scanner consistency. Harmonized images were compared to baseline (no harmonization).

sec.title: Baseline Images
sec.p: Baseline images were defined as those undergoing no harmonization and were used for comparison to the harmonization methods. For these images, skull-stripping using the HD-BET brain extraction tool (14) was performed subsequently after the pre-processing steps described above. Baseline images were intensity normalized to a standardized range of [0,1] using min-max normalization.

sec.title: ComBat Harmonization
sec.p: ComBat is a statistical batch-effect correction tool that incorporates an empirical Bayesian framework to improve the parameter estimate variance (8). In this process, images were first flattened into voxel intensity arrays and organized into a data matrix. The data matrix was structured across rows representing image voxel intensities and columns being each individual subject, and the associated scanner vendor was included as the batch vector to correct for. Using the data from the target batch (the Siemens independent reference group) as the reference standard, these voxel arrays were harmonized by scaling the voxel values through a location and scale (mean and variance) adjustment to account for both additive and multiplicative site effects (8). Age, sex, and cognitive status information were preserved as biological covariate inputs for ComBat. After harmonization, the images were skull-stripped using HD-BET (14). Post-ComBat images were re-intensity normalized using min-max for subsequent comparative analysis.

sec.title: HACA3 Harmonization
sec.p: HACA3 is a recently developed deep learning model for multi-contrast MR harmonization incorporating anatomy disentanglement where the model attempts to separate anatomical imaging data from scanner effects (4). HACA3 incorporates an artifact encoder and attention module which analyzes learned representations of contrast, anatomy, and artifacts (4). The T1w and T2-FLAIR images were padded to the dimensions of 192×224×192 to fit the required HACA3 input data structure, then unpadded post-harmonization. The target image template was created by averaging all images across the reference Siemens cohort. Then, the harmonized images underwent skull-stripping using HD-BET and intensity normalization for standardization of post-processed images in the subsequent analyses. Information about all processing pipelines is shown in Figure 2a.

sec.title: Image Metrics
sec.p: In MRI images, gray matter (GM) and white matter (WM) have different signal intensity, dark in gray matter and bright in white matter in T1w images and vice versa in T2-FLAIR images. Gray/white matter contrast should be consistent across scanners with little variation attributed to individual subject anatomy, particularly in group analyses of similar subjects.

Global gray matter/white matter contrast ratio (G/W ratio) for each subject’s T1w and co-registered T2-FLAIR images were calculated using the average intensities of the GM and WM masks of the T1w image derived from the FSL FMRIB’s Automated Segmentation Tool (FAST) (15). This evaluation was conducted across the image pipeline, both pre- and post-harmonization (Figure 2b). The value of G/W ratio indicates greater tissue contrast delineation when the ratio is further from 1.0, with G/W ratio of T1w expected to be <1.0 and G/W ratio of T2-FLAIR to be >1.0.

Multiple similarity metrics were computed to assess the visual similarity of the images before and after harmonization. A deep feature metrics was chosen to improve the interpretability of establishing improved image similarity.

Fréchet inception distance (FID) is a measure of the similarity of feature distributions between two image datasets from a pre-trained network such as InceptionV3 (16). Unlike the structural similarity index measure (SSIM) and learned perceptual image patch similarity (LPIPS), which measure similarity between a pair of images of the same object, FID can be calculated between unpaired data groups, as this metric evaluates dataset feature distributions. Lower values represent more similar distributions. To implement FID calculation with MRI images, we extracted 2D axial slices from each 3D image scan, which were then padded to 299×299 and converted to a torch tensor to match the InceptionV3 input requirements. For Part 1 of the study, we calculated the dataset FID between each pair of scanners.

In the analysis of paired subject multivendor scans, we calculated LPIPS, a metric using deep features as a measure of perceptual similarity with lower values representing greater similarity (17). This metric was calculated via the AlexNet network architecture to compare the similarity of within-subject multi-vendor sessions in Part 2 of the study.

A common downstream task involving T2-FLAIR images in neurodegenerative disease subjects is the segmentation of white matter hyperintensity (WMH) lesions. This was performed using LST-AI, an AI-based automated WMH segmentation tool (18). For Part 1, total WMH volume (in cc) was calculated and reported as the natural log of the volume (logWMH). We assume here that given the size of the cohorts scanned on each of the three scanners and the similar age and cognitive status distributions, the WMH volumes should not vary by scanner type. Therefore, post-harmonization, we assessed whether there was a smaller spread in mean WMH volumes between scanners, while also evaluating the preservation of the known relationship between increasing WMH and increasing age (19, 20). In Part 2 of the study with paired subject data, we calculated the Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) to evaluate the consistency of white matter hyperintensity segmentation masks across scans of the same subject (21).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Group scanner differences (G/W ratio, logWMH) were evaluated via a one-way ANOVA and post-hoc Games-Howell test. A repeated measures ANOVA and post-hoc paired t-tests with Bonferroni correction were also conducted to compare similarity metrics across pre- and post-harmonization for the multi-vendor subjects. Pearson’s correlation coefficient was used to assess the correlation between WMH volumes and age. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05.

sec.title: Gray Matter/White Matter Contrast Ratio: T1w Imaging
sec.p: Box plots of G/W ratio across all processing pipelines for T1w are shown in Figure 3a. At baseline, the T1w G/W ratio was significantly different across groups, with GE (0.73±0.02) significantly lower (indicating stronger G/W contrast) compared to Philips (0.76±0.02) and Siemens (0.77±0.02). For the subsequent harmonization pipelines, we harmonized all three scanner groups to an independent Siemens group of subjects with a mean G/W ratio for T1w of 0.76±0.02.

After ComBat, the T1w G/W ratios were more consistent across scanner groups and not significantly different from each other (ANOVA p = 0.069) with GE changing the most of the scanner groups compared to baseline (GE: 0.77±0.02, Philips: 0.77±0.02, Siemens: 0.77±0.02).

After HACA3 harmonization, the T1w G/W ratio still had significant differences (GE: 0.77±0.01, Philips: 0.78±0.01, Siemens: 0.77±0.01). However, as for ComBat, GE’s T1w contrast changed significantly from baseline and more closely matched the contrast ratio range of the Philips and Siemens scans (Figure 3a).

sec.title: Gray Matter/White Matter Contrast Ratio: T2-FLAIR Imaging
sec.p: For T2-FLAIR images, the G/W ratio was significantly different across scanners (GE: 0.98±0.05, Philips: 1.04±0.04, Siemens: 1.13±0.06) (Figure 3a). Because their G/W ratio for T2-FLAIR imaging was close to 1, GE and Philips T2-FLAIR images had minimal delineation of gray and white matter compared with Siemens. T2-FLAIR image examples of a randomly selected subject from each scanner group are shown in Figure 4 pre- and post-harmonization. For the subsequent harmonization pipelines, we harmonized all 3 scanner groups to an independent Siemens group of subjects with a mean G/W ratio for T2-FLAIR of 1.14±0.06.

For ComBat, the G/W ratio increased across all groups from baseline (closer to the target contrast), but significant differences persisted across groups (GE: 1.06±0.06, Philips: 1.14±0.08, Siemens: 1.12±0.05). While helping to reduce scanner group mean G/W ratio differences and bring scanner groups closer to the target contrast, ComBat also led to an increase in variance and outliers. A visual example of ComBat overadjustment in contrast is shown in Supplementary Figure S1.

For HACA3, the G/W ratio remained significantly different across groups but had increased G/W delineations across all groups compared to baseline (GE: 1.06±0.03, Philips: 1.12±0.02, Siemens: 1.16±0.03). There was reduced variance in G/W contrast ratio after HACA3, as shown by the smaller interquartile range, which is indicative of higher image contrast consistency.

Games-Howell post-hoc pairwise comparison of group differences across all image sequences and pipelines is shown in Supplementary Figure S2, confirming reductions in scanner contrast differences post-harmonization while moving G/W ratio closer to the target contrast.

sec.title: Feature Similarity
sec.p: We measured lower FID values, which denotes higher feature distribution similarity, post-harmonization compared to the baseline for both T1w and T2-FLAIR images, with HACA3 leading to the highest similarity in most cases (Figure 3b). HACA3 had the best performance for this metric in most cases.

sec.title: White Matter Hyperintensity Volume Comparison
sec.p: Box plots of logWMH volume by scanner across all processing pipelines is shown in Figure 5a. The expectation was to have more similar logWMH distributions across scanner groups post-harmonization since the datasets were age matched and had similar distributions of cognitive status. We found that while harmonization tools led to some degree of improved inter-scanner consistency, the logWMH volumes also had large changes after each harmonization method. Additionally, visual analysis of individual subjects’ T2-FLAIR images indicated there could be potential reduced WMH contrast post-HACA3 harmonization (Figure 6).

At baseline, total logWMH volume was significantly different across scanner vendor groups (GE: 1.18±1.90, Philips: 0.33±2.27, Siemens: 1.04±1.81), with Philips significantly lower compared to the other scanners. After ComBat harmonization, significant differences were still found (GE: 0.87±1.76, Philips: 1.40±1.67, Siemens: 0.79±1.87), with Philips subjects now having significantly higher WMH volumes to the other two groups. Post-HACA3 harmonization, there were no significant differences between the scanner groups’ logWMH volume (ANOVA p = 0.083) (GE: 1.40±1.36, Philips: 1.05±1.82, Siemens: 1.15±1.71). Post-hoc differences between scanner groups are also shown in Figure 5b.

The correlation between age and logWMH volumes across all scanners combined is shown in Figure 7. We found that HACA3 harmonization led to a slightly higher r value (0.412) in the linear regression compared to baseline (0.393) and with ComBat (0.381), though these changes were not statistically significant (Steiger’s Z-test: Baseline vs ComBat p-corr = 1.00, Baseline vs HACA3 p-corr = 0.71, HACA3 vs ComBat p-corr = 0.23). The strongest correlation between logWMH and age was seen in the reference group (0.496) of subjects all scanned on the same scanner vendor (Siemens).

sec.title: PART 2 – Within-Subject Comparison
sec.p: Analysis of paired multi-vendor scan sessions found similar results to Part 1 of the study, again with the most notable visual differences found in T2-FLAIR images at baseline. Consistent with our group analysis results, G/W ratio for paired session T1w and T2-FLAIR images at baseline was different between vendors (with GE being the outlier). Both harmonization pipelines led to more consistent G/W ratios across scanners (Table II). A comparison of paired multi-vendor scan sessions of T1w and T2-FLAIR imaging for one subject pre- and post-harmonization is shown in Figure 8.

Additionally, for all 8 subjects, LPIPS was significantly lower (higher similarity) for only HACA3 harmonization for T1w (Baseline: 0.042±0.011; ComBat: 0.033±0.008 [comparing to baseline, post-hoc p-corr = 0.078]; HACA3: 0.033±0.010 [post-hoc p-corr = 0.013]). Similar findings were seen for T2-FLAIR imaging, with significantly lower LPIPS scores for HACA3 harmonization (Baseline: 0.066±0.013; ComBat: 0.053±0.019 [post-hoc p-corr = 0.439]; HACA3: 0.048±0.013, [post-hoc p-corr = 0.004]). When evaluating WMH segmentation volume, we found non-significant changes (ANOVA p = 0.218; DSC Baseline: 0.43±0.24; ComBat: 0.43±0.20, HACA3: 0.56±0.18). Similarity metrics are further detailed in Table II.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Harmonizing imaging contrast across different scanners and protocols is important to ensure consistency for multi-center studies or studies that change parameters over time (3–5, 10, 22). For example, a previous ADNI paper found significant mean differences in WMH volumes between ADNI protocol phases (23). Current harmonization tools offer the potential to reduce scanner-based differences in MR images, but these methods also come with challenges. While many ML harmonization tools have validated their performance on mostly T1w scans (3), we also evaluated the effects of harmonization on T2-FLAIR images. Both T1w and T2-FLAIR are standard sequences collected in clinical practice for disease pathology evaluation (24). While T1w scans are useful for cortical volume segmentation, T2-FLAIR images are typically used to evaluate changes in WMH volume, which is a marker of vascular disease (25). The harmonization of multi-contrast MR data is especially applicable with foundational models, which seek to use all available data for each subject. In this study, we evaluated quantitative differences in image similarity across scanner vendors in a large public, multicenter study after applying two publicly available harmonization tools. Though a lack of standardized comparison measures exist, we utilized multiple previously recommended harmonization metrics (3, 5), including a combination of image-level feature similarity indices (FID, LPIPS) and downstream analysis of parameters that are expected to be preserved across scanners for large groups of similarly selected patients (global G/W ratio, WMH segmentation). Further, we elaborate the differences between statistical and deep learning approaches, concluding that the deep learning-based HACA3 algorithm generally outperformed the statistical ComBat method.

For the large group analysis, we observed significant differences in G/W contrast ratio between scanner types, that often persisted despite efforts at harmonization. We also saw worse performance for both algorithms for harmonizing T2-FLAIR images. This may be due to differences in how different vendors implement this sequence to optimize CSF suppression while maintaining appropriate parenchymal contrast. For example, for a fast-spin echo sequence such as the ADNI3 T2-FLAIR, changes in the echo train length lead to off-resonance effects that can change the image contrast through mechanisms such as magnetization transfer (26). While imperfect at completely removing contrast differences, both harmonization tools still led to G/W contrast ratios that more closely matched the target contrast and improved consistency between scanner groups. Though ComBat brought G/W ratio values closer to the reference group, we found HACA3 harmonization reduced variance, showcasing improved consistency within each group. Conversely, ComBat had increased contrast outliers for T2-FLAIR images, indicating that this method may result in occasional overadjustments and thus may be unreliable when harmonizing across an entire dataset. Using the FID metric to evaluate image feature distribution similarities, we observed that HACA3 harmonization performed the best. This is particularly relevant for the generalizability of deep learning models, which assume that multicenter datasets have similar feature distributions.

As white matter abnormalities may be closely associated with vascular cognitive impairment (25), we chose automated white matter segmentation as a downstream task to evaluate pathological information consistency across the dataset. We observed that HACA3 performed the best in reducing vendor differences of WMH volumes, but some trends of scanner group differences persisted. Additionally, looking at the relationship between age and WMH, we found that HACA3 led to slightly increased correlation strength and reduced variance in volumes, indicative of more consistent measurements. This suggests that HACA3’s deep learning methods may better preserve complex image information during harmonization. However, it was noted that the application of both harmonization methods caused significant differences in WMH volumes compared to non-harmonized images, which was unexpected, and suggests a degree of instability in the algorithms. These changes could reflect either the sensitivity of automated WMH segmentation or the effects of the harmonization procedures.

Additionally, we analyzed subjects who had scans across multiple scanner vendors within short intervals, allowing us to visually compare paired within-subject scan sessions. While there were relatively few subjects who met the inclusion criteria, we found that harmonization improved image similarity and derived metric consistency, supporting the group-level findings while demonstrating that harmonization tools have benefits in longitudinal scan sessions where sites or protocols may change over time. However, limitations for this analysis involve the small volumes of the white matter lesion masks and small sample size.

Voxel-based harmonization offers the potential to strengthen both manual and automated observations of pathological patterns and improve the performance of deep learning models by reducing domain shift (3, 22). ComBat was chosen as a statistical method comparison due to its known application in harmonization of imaging data features in prior literature and code availability (7–9). However, traditional statistical methods have remained less developed for image-level harmonization, with challenges in preserving the complexity of anatomical images during processing (27). This is demonstrated by ComBat, where we observed group-wise improvement in G/W ratio harmonization, but also an increase in outlier contrasts, resulting in variable image quality post-harmonization. Our findings with ComBat are consistent with the mixed results reported in the literature when applying this method in voxel-level harmonization (28, 29). Deep learning-based methods have also been developed for image harmonization (4, 10, 11). We chose HACA3 for our study evaluation, which is a recent deep learning method that does not require “traveling subject” data, in which the same participants must be scanned on multiple scanners within a short interval, which can be expensive and impractical, and is generally not available with retrospective datasets. Unlike prior deep learning-based methods, HACA3 is pretrained on a robust cohort of subjects from public datasets (OASIS3, BLSA, IXI) and does not require a traveling subject training set (4, 10, 30).

Although we saw overall group improvements in image consistency following HACA3 harmonization, we observed individual cases that appeared to lose pathological information, primarily WMH lesions, as noted in a previous study (4). Harmonization tools may improve image contrast and feature distribution consistency, but care must be taken in application with full clinical datasets, where disease pathology may be at risk of being altered. Additionally, models like HACA3 center around the concept of anatomy disentanglement with the belief that anatomy can be separated from the scanner effects, which can be controversial due to difficulty in defining a vendor-neutral reference scanner. In theory, the choice of the reference scanner type should not be a critical decision, as long as sufficient reference scans from that scanner are available. Practically, it makes sense to harmonize to the most common scanner in your dataset, since this would presumably lead to less overall alteration of the dataset due to the harmonization algorithm.

Specific harmonization tool input requirements also remain a limitation. For example, HACA3 requires specific pre-processing such as inhomogeneity correction and spatial normalization, limiting applicability when needing analysis of raw images. Moreover, differences in chosen pre-processing methods and baseline contrast can affect how the images are harmonized. The input batch of a large image reference sample in ComBat versus HACA3’s averaged target image input can also limit the interpretability of our comparison.

Another approach to harmonization is to optimize parameters across scanner types more thoroughly. For example, a contrast-optimized protocol for a GE scanner with adjusted parameters (longer echo time, inversion time, repetition time) compared to the ADNI4 protocol greatly improved the delineations of gray and white matter on T2-FLAIR images such that it was more similar to a Siemens scan (31). While large-scale studies attempt to specify consistent imaging protocols, individual vendors and scanners may implement these parameters differently, leading to differences in image appearance. In designing sequence parameters, we suggest an emphasis on optimizing feature similarities such as FID and LPIPS for better harmonization across sites. However, it remains to be seen how such changes this may affect downstream analyses such as with WMH volumes. Additionally, future acquisition changes offer no benefit for previously collected data, and protocols generally require accommodation for the least advanced imaging systems.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: This work assessed only two harmonization methods due to their publicly availability. We also evaluated only two common sequence types, neglecting other contrasts that may also be of interest. This study used data from a large, publicly available neuroimaging dataset; while this allows others to reproduce our results, the protocols chosen in ADNI3 were specific to that study and results may differ for other datasets with different scanner protocols. Finally, the subject-level evaluation is limited by the small number of participants who had scans on different scanners within a short time frame.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Overall, we found that HACA3, a retrospective deep-learning harmonization tool, outperformed a statistical method (ComBat) in the large ADNI3 neuroimaging dataset. However, both methods have challenges, including pre-processing requirements and instability for WMH volume segmentation. Ultimately, attention to protocol optimization across scanners, ideally using deep feature metrics to assess similarity, as well as post-processing harmonization methods, should be considered together to improve the value of large, multi-center imaging datasets.

caption.p: Figure S1: Example of ComBat T2-FLAIR contrast overadjustment. T2-FLAIR scans of a Philips subject (CN, male, 75.2 years) across harmonization pipelines. G/W contrast: Baseline (1.09), ComBat (1.46). HACA3 (1.15). LogWMH volumes: Baseline (3.74), ComBat (3.63), HACA3 (3.67).

Table S2: G/W ratio Games-Howell post-hoc comparisons between scanner groups. Post-Hoc Games Howell test results including 95% confidence intervals of the mean differences between scanner groups and significance are shown for each scanner pair combination at baseline and post-harmonization. We can observe that statistically significant differences often remain post-harmonization. However, the mean G/W contrast ratio differences are small, especially when compared to baseline, indicating a shift in contrast closer to harmonization.

ack.title: Acknowledgements:
ack.p: Data collection and sharing for this project was funded by the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) (National Institutes of Health Grant U01 AG024904) and DOD ADNI (Department of Defense award number W81XWH-12–2-0012). ADNI is funded by the National Institute on Aging, the National Institute of Biomedical Imaging and Bioengineering, and through generous contributions from the following: AbbVie, Alzheimer’s Association; Alzheimer’s Drug Discovery Foundation; Araclon Biotech; BioClinica, Inc.; Biogen; Bristol-Myers Squibb Company; CereSpir, Inc.; Cogstate; Eisai Inc.; Elan Pharmaceuticals, Inc.; Eli Lilly and Company; EuroImmun; F. Hoffmann-La Roche Ltd and its affiliated company Genentech, Inc.; Fujirebio; GE Healthcare; IXICO Ltd.; Janssen Alzheimer Immunotherapy Research & Development, LLC.; Johnson & Johnson Pharmaceutical Research & Development LLC.; Lumosity; Lundbeck; Merck & Co., Inc.; Meso Scale Diagnostics, LLC.; NeuroRx Research; Neurotrack Technologies; Novartis Pharmaceuticals Corporation; Pfizer Inc.; Piramal Imaging; Servier; Takeda Pharmaceutical Company; and Transition Therapeutics. The Canadian Institutes of Health Research is providing funds to support ADNI clinical sites in Canada. Private sector contributions are facilitated by the Foundation for the National Institutes of Health (www.fnih.org). The grantee organization is the Northern California Institute for Research and Education, and the study is coordinated by the Alzheimer’s Therapeutic Research Institute at the University of Southern California. ADNI data are disseminated by the Laboratory for Neuro Imaging at the University of Southern California.

sec.title: Grant Support:
sec.p: This study was supported by NIH grant P30AG066515-06.

fn.p: Conflict of Interest: Dr. Greg Zaharchuk reported receiving royalties from Cambridge University Press; travel support and honoraria for lectures from Bracco; various patents; equity in Subtle Medical. Dr. Elizabeth Mormino has been a paid consultant for Roche, Genentech, Eli Lilly, and Neurotrack. The other authors declare no competing interests.

* Data used in preparation of this article were obtained from the Alzheimer’s Disease Neuroimaging Initiative (ADNI) database (adni.loni.usc.edu). As such, the investigators within the ADNI contributed to the design and implementation of ADNI and/or provided data but did not participate in analysis or writing of this report. A complete listing of ADNI investigators can be found at: http://adni.loni.usc.edu/wp-content/uploads/how_to_apply/ADNI_Acknowledgement_List.pdf

caption.p: Flow chart of subject scan session inclusion from the ADNI3 dataset. For Part 1, only 1 scan session per subject was included from ADNI3, with both T1w and T2-FLAIR sequences available from that session. For Part 2, subjects were included only if they had scans performed on different scanner vendors within a 3-year period. Visual quality control was done on all images pre- and post-processing, leading to one scan being excluded due to processing failures in Part 1.

Study design setup comparing scanner vendor differences in the ADNI3 dataset, pre- and post-harmonization. (a) Each ADNI T1w and T2-FLAIR image underwent standardized pre-processing and pipeline-specific steps to satisfy harmonization tool input requirements. (b) Post-processed images were quantitatively assessed for scanner-based differences across baseline and harmonization pipelines via various image-based metrics (global gray/white-matter contrast ratio [G/W ratio], Fréchet inception distance [FID], and white matter hyperintensity [WMH] segmentation).

(a) Box plots of T1w and T2-FLAIR G/W ratio for all scanner groups (GE, Philips, Siemens), split by processing pipeline (Baseline, ComBat, HACA3). When harmonizing to a Siemens independent reference group, we observed noticeable increases in G/W ratio across all scanner groups, improving inter-scanner contrast consistency and moving the contrast ratio values closer to the Siemens reference group contrast (T1w: 0.76, T2-FLAIR: 1.14). (b) FID scores across all pairs of scanner datasets. We observed decreased FID (higher feature distribution similarity) post-harmonization, with the most improvement in scanner vendor data similarity after HACA3 processing.

Representative T2-FLAIR images from each scanner vendor at baseline and post-harmonization with ComBat and HACA3. At baseline, there are visible contrast differences across the 3 scans of different vendors, with GE having the lowest gray/white matter contrast and Siemens having the highest. After harmonization to a Siemens reference group, there is more consistent gray/white contrast delineation, as well as preserved individual anatomical features including white matter hyperintensities.

(a) Box plots of logWMH volumes by scanner group at baseline, post-ComBat, and post-HACA3. We see more consistent volumes across scanners post-HACA3 harmonization (ANOVA p = 0.083). However, as shown by the differences in mean logWMH across processing pipelines, automated WMH segmentation can be highly sensitive to the choice of post-processing algorithm. (b) Post-hoc differences and statistical significance between scanner groups of logWMH volumes are shown below each corresponding box plot for each processing pipeline.

Example of WMH segmentation changes post-harmonization. T2-FLAIR images of a Philips subject (MCI, female, 64.1 years) showcase reduced WMH contrast and corresponding WMH segmentation mask post-HACA3.

Scatterplots of logWMH volumes versus age across all scanners. There is a slightly stronger linear relationship with HACA3 images (r = 0.412) compared to baseline (r = 0.393). logWMH volumes from HACA3 images also led to the closest Pearson correlation coefficient to the reference Siemens only group (r = 0.496). Post-ComBat, there was no improved correlation strength observed (r = 0.381). As expected, the correlation between logWMH and age was highest for the reference cohort (r = 0.496) who were all scanned on the same scanners (Siemens).

Consecutive scan sessions (GE 2018 and Siemens 2019) of are displayed for an ADNI3 subject (MCI, female, age at scan [years]: 72.2 (GE) and 73.4 (Siemens)) both before and after harmonization. There is improved gray-white matter consistency for T2-FLAIR images post-harmonization, as shown by the accompanying image metrics. For this subject, ComBat better harmonized the G/W ratio for T2-FLAIR, while HACA3 led to improved WMH segmentation consistency. Both pipelines performed similarly for T1w harmonization.

caption.title: Demographics.
caption.p: Demographics of the included subjects for both Part 1 (a) and Part 2 (b) of the study. (a) Subjects are split by scanner vendor, including GE, Philips, the age-matched Siemens sample, and the independent reference Siemens group. (b) Characteristics of the multi-vendor subjects with scan sessions across different scanners are displayed.

caption.title: Image Metrics of Multi-Vendor Subject Scans.
caption.p: T1w/T2-FLAIR summary metrics of the 8 multi-vendor subjects, split by the scanner pair of their two scan sessions as well as summarized across all 8 subjects in the final table. G/W contrast ratio, WMH DSC, and LPIPS scores are shown.
